reason: The item largely stays on a single admissibility axis (what can/can’t be concluded from comparative overpressure/velocity statements), and the correct selections are mostly bounded to the given observations. However, several options introduce unscoped absolutes (“under all pipeline conditions,” “guarantees,” “cannot be dismissed”) that create avoidable gray-zone debates about generality and engineering thresholds, reducing boundary tightness and discriminative separation. Decoys are plausible but two rely on similar overgeneralization/overreach patterns, which can weaken single-fault purity and expert-level discrimination.

The item cleanly tests boundary-aware inference from asymmetric overpressure measurements and a stated damage threshold to admissible hazard-zone conclusions, while penalizing causal mechanism overclaims and misuse of thresholds. Decoys are engineering-plausible and mostly single-fault, requiring experts to separate descriptive inferences (zones/risk asymmetry) from unsupported causal attribution and zero-risk claims. Some residual ambiguity exists because option D leans on an inferred causal chain (opening pressure → energy accumulation → far-field overpressure) that could be seen as interpretive rather than purely entailed by the measurement statement, slightly limiting discriminative sharpness.

As a SHORT_ANSWER item, there are no explicit answer options/decoys to audit, which collapses decoy craftsmanship and materially weakens discriminative benchmarking under the provided rubric. While the prompt targets two coherent mechanisms (vent activation threshold asymmetry and inert dilution effects), it invites broad, partially overlapping explanations (directionality, vent area, impulse, geometry) and thus risks boundary ambiguity and inconsistent grading without tightly specified scoring anchors beyond the must-include list.
